Document [0] (Title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: HVLA Procedure - Lumbar Vertebrae -- Contraindications) Relative contra-indications would necessitate a discussion with the patient about the possible risks and benefits before the treatment application to inform them of specific risks associated with each condition. Absolute contraindications include osteoporosis, osteomyelitis, fractures in the area of thrusting, boney metastasis, severe rheumatoid arthritis (especially true in cervical HVLA), cauda equina/conus medularis syndrome (or symptoms of numbness/weakness suggestive of these diagnoses), fused spinal joints, osteomyelitis, joint instability, Down syndrome (also especially true in cervical spine manipulation), and patient refusal. Typically, the presence of an absolute contraindication excludes HVLA, as the risks would heavily outweigh any benefit. [6] [4]

Document [10] (Title: Anterior Inferior Iliac Spine Avulsion -- Pearls and Other Issues) AIIS avulsion treatment depends on various factors, including fracture severity and the patient's age and activity level. Conservative approaches, such as rest, activity modification, and physical therapy, are typically effective in many cases, facilitating satisfactory outcomes. However, surgical intervention may be warranted in cases of significant fragment displacement or failed conservative management. Recent advancements in surgical techniques, particularly minimally invasive approaches like arthroscopy, have greatly improved outcomes by focusing on reattaching the avulsed fragment and restoring normal anatomy. Despite its potential benefits, surgery carries inherent risks such as heterotopic ossification and nerve damage, emphasizing the importance of careful patient selection and surgical technique to minimize complications and promote optimal recovery.

Document [29] (Title: Epidural Steroid Injections -- Indications) Another common indication for epidural steroid injection is nerve root irritation secondary to spinal stenosis. Document [17] (Title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431) Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment modality for several specific orthopedic injuries. It is considered a type of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity excluding the hip and femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.
Document [18] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755) The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.
